title: Lemon Syllabub
subtitle: Double cream whipped with lemon, sugar, and a little sweet wine
excerpt: Double cream whipped with lemon zest and juice, caster sugar, and a measure of sweet sherry to a thick, mousse-like consistency. Served in glasses without setting, the cream is just firm enough to spoon but not stiff. A cold British dessert that takes ten minutes to make.
sourceNotes: Syllabub is one of the oldest English dessert preparations, with versions appearing in cookery manuscripts from the sixteenth and seventeenth centuries as a sweet spiced drink made with cream and wine. The cold set version, where cream is whipped with wine and lemon juice to a thick foam, became standard in the eighteenth century and is the form still made today.

content.text: Syllabub is cream whipped with the acid and flavour of lemon and wine. The acid in the lemon juice slightly destabilises the cream as it whips, giving the finished syllabub a particular texture, less airy than plain whipped cream, slightly heavier, with a more pronounced flavour from the moment it hits the spoon. It doesn't set firm; the consistency should be soft enough to spoon into a glass, not stiff enough to pipe.

The wine here is sweet sherry (Pedro Ximenez or oloroso), which adds a deep, slightly caramel note that works well against the brightness of the lemon. The sherry can be omitted or swapped for a dry white wine if preferred, which gives a lighter, sharper result.
Stir the lemon zest and juice together with {{caster-sugar}} of caster sugar and {{sherry-sweet}} of sweet sherry (if using) in a large bowl. Leave for 10 minutes to dissolve the sugar.

Pour {{double-cream}} of double cream into the lemon and sugar mixture. Whip with a hand mixer or a whisk on medium speed until the mixture just holds soft peaks, the cream should be thick enough to mound in a spoon but not stiff. Stop before it reaches firm peaks; the syllabub should be soft and mousse-like.
Spoon into serving glasses and chill for at least an hour before serving. Serve with a strip of lemon zest on top and shortbread or biscotti alongside.

items.fix: Chill the cream and bowl before whipping. Continue whipping if the syllabub looks loose, it should just hold its shape.
items.cause: Cream was whipped too little, or cream temperature was too warm
items.symptom: Syllabub is too thin and pourable

items.fix: Stop whipping at soft peaks, the acid from the lemon makes cream prone to over-whipping into a grainy texture. Serve within 6 hours of making.
items.cause: Over-whipped, or left for too long before serving
items.symptom: Syllabub has separated, with liquid at the bottom of the glass

items.fix: Stir in another tablespoon of caster sugar before whipping. Taste the mixture before adding the cream, it should taste pleasantly sharp, not mouth-puckering.
items.cause: Too much lemon juice relative to sugar
items.symptom: Flavour is very sharp and unbalanced

items.fix: The cream has been taken too far and cannot be recovered. For next time, stop as soon as the cream holds soft peaks, a syllabub should not be stiff.
items.cause: Over-whipped to the point where the fat in the cream has started to separate

content.text: Syllabub is one of the oldest English desserts with a continuous history. Early versions from the sixteenth and seventeenth centuries were warm drinks. Cream was poured from a height into a bowl of spiced wine, and the frothy head was drunk on its own. By the eighteenth century the whipped version had become standard: cream whipped with wine and lemon and served cold in glasses. The Victorian household manual by Mrs Beeton recorded the dish. It faded through the twentieth century, then came back in British restaurants and home cooking. Cooks like it because it is simple and made ahead.
